- Tried strategies: quit during a fast period → did not help
[Recent Conversation]

Therapist: Now that you've planned a way to involve Durga and your great-grandson, how do you think you'll feel when you don't have paan to turn to in those afternoons, and what might be a challenging part of this new plan for you?

Patient: I don't know... I think I'll be okay without paan when there's someone to keep me company. But maybe I'll feel a little bored or restless if I don't have my own time to relax. And what if my son or daughter-in-law say something about the smoke and the paan smell? They've already mentioned it.
Therapist: In this phase, I need to focus on introducing a coping strategy or technique that seems relevant to the patient's specific triggers and past attempts.
Here's a response that fits this phase:
Therapist: To deal with those moments of boredom or restlessness, what if you set aside a small space in your home for a quiet activity, like drawing or reading, that you can enjoy without paan?

Patient: I don't know... I'm not very good at drawing or reading. Maybe I can try to knit or crochet something for my great-grandson. My sister used to make all these beautiful blankets and clothes for us when we were young. I remember liking it when she did things like that.
Therapist: This is the Planning phase, where I'm supposed to introduce a coping strategy or technique. Since the patient has expressed interest in knitting or crochet, I'll build on that.
Therapist: Would you be willing to set aside a specific time each day, even just 15 minutes, to work on that knitting or crochet project, so it becomes a regular activity to look forward to?

Patient: Yes, I can do that. My sister always said the key is to make something small every day, so it becomes a habit. I'll put my yarn and needles on the same table where I used to keep my paan, that way I'll remember to go there.

step7_response: Now that you've associated knitting or crochet with your sister's love, how might you feel about having a tangible, handmade gift to show your great-grandson, which could also be a reminder of your progress towards quitting paan?

turns.user_message: That's a nice thought. When I see my great-grandson's face light up when he gets something I made, it would remind me of why I wanted to quit paan in the first place. It's for him, and I want to be healthy for him. I'll make something nice, something special, just for him.
